data.title: Canada Unemployment Rate Surges To 7.1% In August
data.description:
The unemployment rate in Canada has risen to 7.1% in August, the highest level since May 2016 outside of the pandemic era, Statistics Canada (StatCan) reported Friday.

The surge in the jobless rate was driven by a 66,000 drop in employment in August, which followed a 41,000 decline in July. The labor force also shrank in the last two months, bringing the labor force participation rate down by 0.3 percentage points since June to 65.1%.

Full-time employment accounted for 6,000 of the total, while decline in part-time positions represented the majority of the drop at 59,700. Outside of the pandemic era, August marked the first time since Nov 2019 that job losses occurred in both full-time and part-time employment at the same month.

Professional, scientific and technical services (-26,100), transportation and warehousing (-22,700), manufacturing (-19,200) as well as education (-18,400) sectors all cut a substantial number of jobs in the month. These job losses were only partially offset by the solid employment growth in construction (17,100) and accommodation and food services (9,200) industries.

This job report reveals an apparent weakness in the Canadian labor market that will likely drag down consumption demand—one of the few remaining bright spots in Q2 GDP report—impacting the third quarter’s economic growth.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UiPath: Proving Agentic AI Proficiency And Staging A Comeback
data.description:
Investing in the AI era has been incredibly tough this year. Almost every single tech company is claiming proficiency in deploying AI solutions, but there appears to be a clear divide between the stocks that investors have deemed as winners and those that have been marked as losers. On the one hand, there are mega-caps like Palantir (PLTR) that seemingly only move in one direction: up. On the other hand, other stocks like C3.ai (AI) and even cloud titans like Salesforce (CRM) have been tossed into the penalty box.

UiPath (NYSE:PATH), in my view, is attempting to make a crossover into the ascendant territory. The company just reported Q2 results that showcased a sharp acceleration in revenue growth and a healthy boost to full-year guidance, signaling increased adoption for its agentic AI-focused products.

I last wrote a bullish article on UiPath in July, when the stock was trading higher at ~$12 per share. Since then, UiPath has seen soaring growth while also padding its margin profile and bolstering its free cash flow story. Its ARR has continued to rise, adding to the durability of its revenue base. As such, I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion on the stock.

To me, these are the core reasons to be long on UiPath:

  • UiPath has long focused on agentic AI, which enjoys secular tailwinds. Almost every software company has released some kind of AI tool, but most feature copilot or "intelligent recommendations" that don't offer true automation or labor-saving benefits. UiPath's agentic innovations, meanwhile, reach across every industry and help automate human tasks.
  • Industry recognition. UiPath is classified as one of the top Leaders by the Gartner Magic Quadrant, one of the most influential software industry reviewers.
  • Large and growing ARR base. UiPath's ARR (annualized recurring revenue) is substantially larger than its current-year revenue guidance, indicating a business composed of highly predictable and recurring revenue streams with room for upside. The excess of ARR to guidance also makes it likely for UiPath to keep issuing beat-and-raise quarters.
  • Economies of scale. UiPath is also hitting pro forma operating margins in the mid-teens, which is easy for a company with best-in-class margins in the mid-80%s.
  • Substantial cash reserves. The company's >$1 billion of cash represents nearly a quarter of the company's current market value, and gives UiPath plenty of financial flexibility to chase growth initiatives.

Despite these strengths, we find that UiPath still trades incredibly cheaply, especially for a stock buoyed by AI tailwinds. At current share prices in the mid-$11s, UiPath trades at a market cap of $6.12 billion. After we net off the $1.45 billion of cash (free of debt) on the company's latest balance sheet, UiPath's resulting enterprise value is $4.67 billion.

Meanwhile, UiPath has raised its current-year FY26 (the fiscal year ending in January 2026) revenue outlook to $1.571-$1.576 billion, representing 10% y/y growth, alongside a ~21.6% operating margin for the year. This is substantially higher than the 7% growth and ~18% margin that UiPath originally signaled in its prior guidance.

If we conservatively assume an FCF margin of 23%, flat to FY25 (and consistent with 1H'26 margins, though pro forma operating margins are expected to rise ~5 points this year), FCF would be $362 million.

For next year, FY26, Street estimates are pointing to $1.71 billion in revenue (+8% y/y), and a conservative 23% FCF margin assumption on that revenue profile would indicate $393 million in FCF. This positions UiPath's valuation multiples at:

  • 3.0x EV/FY26 revenue and 2.7x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 12.9x EV/FY26 FCF and 11.9x EV/FY27 FCF

These are incredibly cheap valuation multiples for an AI stock that is seeing revenue growth accelerate, has a large measure of safety in its ARR base, and is seeing soaring operating margins. Stay long here and ride the post-earnings momentum higher.

Q2 download

Let's now go through UiPath's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

UiPath's revenue grew 14% y/y to $361.7 million, blasting past Wall Street's expectations of $347.3 million (+10% y/y) by a four-point margin. UiPath's revenue growth also accelerated 8 points versus 6% y/y growth in Q1, which in turn improved versus 4% y/y growth in Q1.

UiPath's accelerating growth rates are proof of the market size for its agentic AI and automation products. But it isn't just secular tailwinds driving the improvements: UiPath notes as well that it has been modifying its sales organization, incorporating tighter pipeline reviews to generate higher-quality leads and improve closing rates. Per CEO Daniel Dines' remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Importantly, our AI and Agentic solutions are helping us win deals and increase deal sizes faster than traditional automation engagements and now represent a growing share of commercial activity [...]

This momentum reflects the work we've completed to elevate execution. Over the past year, we rebuilt our go-to-market for scale, stabilizing our structure, adding sales capacity and specialists and instituting value-based plays with tighter pipeline inspection. We are seeing the impact: higher-quality pipeline, more predictable forecasting, better customer adoption and implementation and larger multi-solution opportunities, especially where Agentic and IDP land on top of our RPA and API foundation. You can see this execution showing up in the field. We continue to acquire high-quality new logos.

One of the best measures of UiPath's growth is its continued expansion of ARR. In Q2, the company added $30 million in net-new ARR, ending the quarter at $1.72 billion in ARR, up 11% y/y. On top of this, net retention rates are clocking in at 108%, indicating that within this current install base alone, UiPath is trending at ~8% net expansion.

This is a very bullish indicator, especially against very conservative Wall Street estimates for FY27. Just midway into FY26, the company's $1.72 billion in ARR is already greater than Street expectations for FY27 revenue, and that's not even counting the ~8% net expansion that we should expect to see from this existing customer base. In my view, as long as we don't see any sudden spike in churn or slowdown in new deal activity, UiPath should be able to comfortably execute healthy beat-and-raise quarters ahead.

Nor is UiPath ignoring profitability. As its ARR flywheel continues to give the company the benefits of built-in revenue growth without spending substantial incremental sales dollars, the company has capitalized on its 84% pro forma gross margin to generate substantial economies of scale.

In fact, UiPath actually achieved an acceleration to double-digit revenue growth while cutting nominal sales and marketing expenses (on a pro forma basis, excluding stock comp) by 5% y/y to $140.9 million. The company made even deeper cuts in general and administrative spending, down 11% y/y to $40.1 million. UiPath is showing by example the benefits of agentic AI in reducing headcount—unlike many other companies that tout AI automation benefits and yet are doing little to rein in headcount growth (ServiceNow (NOW) comes to mind).

As a result of these efficiency moves, UiPath's pro forma operating margins leaped 15 points y/y to 17%, and the company's guidance for ~22% full-year margins indicates further improvement ahead.

Key takeaways

Agentic AI deal momentum, healthy ARR growth, operating margin expansion driven by the company's own automation initiatives, and a very attractive valuation against revenue/FCF on top of rich cash reserves: There's a lot to like about UiPath exiting the Q2 earnings season. Continue to pay attention to the fact that ARR is well above current-year and forward-year estimates, which is a telling signal that UiPath should continue to blaze past Street expectations. Stay long here.

data.title: Is The Stock Market Overbought?
data.description:
The short answer: Yes, it appears to be. The somewhat longer answer: Yes, but (you knew this was coming) a number of indicators have been advising no less over the summer, and yet the market has continued to climb. Short-term timing decisions, in other words, remain as fraught as ever. But let’s press on anyway and consider some of the clues that imply the market is toppy.

Let’s start by noting that the trend remains red hot. The SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY), a proxy for US equities, continues to exhibit a strong upside trend. As such, the case for arguing the opposite relies on a hefty dose of contrarian thinking. As such, it’s still reasonable to wait for signs of downside momentum before going too far in engaging in hedging.

Yet it’s also clear that market valuation is lofty. The Shiller PE, for instance, shows that the market has been valued at a higher level only once since the late 1800s. That alone doesn’t mean that a correction is imminent, but it’s a reminder that the margin for error is wafer thin re: negative surprises.

Analysts have been making similar points this summer, such as this July 12 headline: “The stock market now is clearly overbought – but overbought does not mean ‘sell’”.

What’s the bottom line? That depends on the investor, along with a host of other factors, such as time horizon, age, risk tolerance, etc. Where you fall along this multi-factor spectrum will influence your decision on how or if to adjust your portfolio, and so generic recommendations definitely do not apply here. A 25-year-old investor with a decades-long horizon can reasonably decide to ignore warning signs. Someone at or in retirement, by contrast, may want to think differently.

Meantime, a metric I monitor for gauging market sentiment tells me that the near-term outlook for the market appears overbought, based on this twist of estimating overbought/oversold conditions for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). According to this indicator, the market is once again in elevated terrain that implies caution for the near-term outlook.

History suggests, although in no way guarantees, that as the Overbought-Oversold Indicator approaches and exceeds +1, the risk-reward prospects are skewed to elevated to the downside for stocks. Although the forecasting record is far from perfect, it’s not terribly surprising that as the indicator is near or above, corrections have been known to follow.

The current reading is 0.86, which is still modestly below +1, and so presumably the bull still has room to run. But if this indicator rises further, investors should consider what could keep the uptrend bubbling. Tech earnings? Economic resilience? Interest rate cuts?

Meanwhile, here’s my take: To the extent that the SPY’s Overbought-Oversold Indicator holds steady or rises from current levels, the market will be increasingly range-bound until/if a new catalyst reignites bullish sentiment.

The market has already priced in a favorable outlook from artificial intelligence and other tech-related drivers that are skewing S&P 500 earnings to the upside. The question, always: What might the incoming news bring, and will that be enough to justify higher valuations and prices?

No one knows, of course, but this much is clear: there’s relatively little room for disappointment at this stage. That alone shouldn’t determine your asset allocation, but it should at least inspire a discussion of what you think you know and what you could be overlooking.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tennant Company: Results Have Worsened, But Upside Remains
data.description:
In June of this year, I decided to revisit Tennant Company (TNC) after seeing the share price of the company underperform compared to my own expectations. You see, in March of this year, I called it a "Buy" candidate. Even though I acknowledged at the time that there were shortcomings with the business, I viewed it as an industry leader with shares that were trading at pretty decent levels. But from that time until I subsequently revisited it in June, the stock dropped 13.6% at a time when the S&P 500 was up 3.5%. I wanted to see if maybe I had this wrong. But in my June article, I argued that the picture for the company was still positive. Admittedly, financial performance has been coming in weaker this year compared to last year. However, at the end of the day, I believed the firm still justified some upside.

From the time that June article was published until today, the stock is up 8.7%. Although it's still not back to where it was, that represents outperformance compared to the S&P 500's 6.5% gain during the same period. Revenue, profits, and some cash flow metrics continue to decline year over year. As a result, the company is more expensive now than it was back then. However, with management still holding firm on guidance for 2025 and the stock trading at levels that are near the low end of what other similar firms are trading for, I believe that it justifies keeping the "Buy" rating I had on it earlier this year.

Tennant will clean up

The only new data that we have for shareholders regarding Tennant Company covers the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that quarter, revenue for the company was a bit disappointing. At $318.6 million, it represented a drop of 3.7% compared to the $331 million the company reported the previous year. This decline in revenue was driven by a 6.3% decline in overall volume. It was only because of price increases that positively affected sales by 1.8% and a favorable foreign currency benefit of 0.8% that the picture wasn't worse than it ultimately was.

According to management, volumes dropped across all geographies in which the company operates. But North America was singled out as the big pain point, following a massive drop in backlog year over year. Overall revenue for North America declined 6.3% year over year from $227.8 million to $213.5 million, though it was not alone in the weakness. While the firm’s EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions reported a rise in sales from $81.5 million to $84.7 million, that was only because of foreign currency fluctuations. Actual organic sales contracted by 1.4% because of weakness in Germany and the Middle East. And in the Asia Pacific region, revenue dropped 6%, from $21.7 million to $20.4 million. Lower volumes in China seem to have been the primary issue here.

This decline in revenue resulted in a decline in profitability as well. Net income fell from $27.9 million last year to $20.2 million this year. The decline in adjusted net profits was from $30.5 million to $25.3 million. Indeed, operating cash flow grew, rising from $18.6 million to $22.5 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a drop from $44.1 million to $39.5 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business declined from $58.6 million to $51 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first half of this year in its entirety compared to the same time last year. And in the chart below, you can see how much volumes, pricing, and foreign currency fluctuations impacted sales.

There are some positives and negatives to consider when it comes to the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety. To be clear, management is forecasting that revenue should go from $1.29 billion last year to between $1.21 billion and $1.25 billion this year. Overall organic revenue should decline by between 1% and 4%. Even though order growth is up, having risen 4% year over year in the most recent quarter, making it the fifth consecutive quarter of solid order performance, management is wary about macroeconomic conditions and, when it comes to China at least, heightened competitive pressures.

Another risk that investors need to be aware of would be tariffs. Management mentioned that their customer base is talking about tariffs, though the company has not yet seen demand signals that would indicate a downturn driven by those specifically. In addition to the prospect that tariffs could reduce demand for customers, management is also forecasting a full-year impact for 2025 of $20 million associated with them. To combat this, management is using certain supply chain actions, changing its procurement strategies, and adjusting the prices on its goods. But with the economic picture already plagued with uncertainty, there's no telling what the ultimate impact will be on its customers.

Despite this, management remains confident that adjusted earnings per share will come in at between $5.70 and $6.20. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of $111.2 million. That would be down, unfortunately, from the $125.5 million the company reported the previous year. Meanwhile, EBITDA is expected to fall from $208.8 million last year to around $202.5 million this year if management hits the midpoint of guidance of between $196 million and $209 million. This should mean an adjusted operating cash flow of approximately $116.4 million.

If we use these estimates and use the historical results the company achieved last year, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These are multiples that would typically teeter between putting the company in the undervalued category and being fairly valued. However, relative to other firms that have similarities to it, the stock is actually quite affordable. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. On both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, I found that Tennant Company ended up being the cheapest of the group. And when it came to the price-to-operating-cash-flow approach, two of the five firms were cheaper.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Tennant Company 13.8 13.1 8.2
Mueller Industries, Inc. (MLI) 15.2 16.0 10.8
Mayville Engineering Company, Inc. (MEC) 17.2 3.8 8.6
SPX Technologies, Inc. (SPXC) 42.0 35.4 22.1
The Timken Company (TKR) 17.5 11.5 9.5
Parker-Hannifin Corporation (PH) 28.0 26.2 21.1

Takeaway

I currently believe investors are wise to be cautious. These are uncertain times, and Tennant Company is already dealing with a lot of uncertainty. Financial performance has weakened, and that certainly makes the business less attractive than it was previously. However, shares of the company are decently priced and actually look attractive compared to other comparable firms. In light of that, I believe that keeping it rated a soft "Buy" is the right choice for now.

data.title: Can Stocks Defy September's Bad Reputation For Markets?
data.description:
September has historically been a tough month for stocks. But things could be different this time around. David Sekera, Chief U.S. Market Strategist with Morningstar Research, says the markets have entered the month trading at fair value. He discusses potential opportunities, especially amongst small-caps longer term.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: We have entered the first week of September. This is a month that has historically been a tough one for stocks. And so far, our next guest-- or our guest, the guest of the day-- says the market's been trading at its fair value. So are there opportunities for investors heading into the fall? David Sekera, Chief US market strategist with Morningstar Research.

Great to see you again, David.

David Sekera: Good afternoon. Greg. How are you doing?

Greg Bonnell: Not too bad. Let's talk about that bad reputation that September has historically made for itself. We're also, you think, at fair value right now. What are the big themes in the market?

David Sekera: Well, we shall see what September ends up bringing us, but it's already been a volatile year. When I think back, we started off the year with the big sell-off and all the overvalued and overextended AI names once DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) hit the headlines.

That was then further exacerbated by the tariffs, which ended up pushing the market down too far, in our view. We actually moved to an overweight recommendation on the April 7 episode of The Morning Filter, which is my weekly podcast. And then since then, the market has rallied after it realized it wasn't going to be the economic apocalypse everyone initially had thought it was going to be. AI spending has really been able to hold up the US economy and the stock market ever since.

So at this point, as you mentioned, now we're back at fair value compared to a composite of the stocks that we cover that trade on US exchanges. So question is, what's really changing? So what we saw in August is really a broadening out of the returns across the market, moving away from those big, mega-cap AI stocks into those areas that had lagged the market to the upside. So we saw small-caps outperform. They are up 4.58% last month, well ahead of large- and mid-caps, which were both up about 2%. And value stocks also caught a bid. They were up over 5%, whereas growth stocks were only up four tenths of a percent.

So I think we're seeing a lot of indications that valuations for those mega-cap growth stocks are getting to be pretty high, and momentum is definitely running out in those areas.

Greg Bonnell: I think when you do end up in a market situation like this-- because it has been choppy-- but we're not that far off of all-time highs for the S&P 500. You feel that the market is fairly valued at this level. You mentioned small-caps. Is this one of the pockets of opportunities? Are there opportunities?

David Sekera: Yeah. I think we've talked about small-caps for probably well over a year now, Greg, every time that we've chatted about the market. And we've always noted that from our valuation standpoint, small-caps were well undervalued, both on an absolute basis as well as a relative value basis. But the investing in small-caps is an investment and not a trade. Now, historically, small-caps tend to do well when you're in an environment where economic growth is starting to re-accelerate after either a slowdown or a recession. The Fed's easing monetary policy and long-term interest rates are coming down. And that had not been the environment for at least the past year, if not past year and a half.

But we thought that investors should be attracted to those small-caps and probably overweight those, because again, you can never really perfectly time when to get in. And the thing I've always noted about small-caps in the past is it doesn't take that much of a rotation out of large- and mid-cap into small-cap for small-caps really to rally very quickly. And if you're not already invested in those small-caps, you could miss it to the upside.

So at this point, what I think the market is looking at is at least two of those three conditions are now being met. So we expect the Fed to start easing here at the September meeting. Our economic team, looking for at least one more cut this year, hundred basis points of cuts to the Fed funds rate over the course of next year. Long-term interest rates have been on a downward trend for the past couple of weeks, if not the past month or so. Our US economics team is projecting an average 3.9% for the US 10-year over the course of 2026, and falling all the way down to 3.5% in 2027.

So that just really leaves the question here, what is the path for the US economy? And unfortunately, that question is extremely hard to answer right now. So if you looked at the headline GDP print in the first quarter, that was negative. It was then positive in the second quarter. That would then meet that third condition that we really need for small-caps to rally. But, of course, that was very distorted by the pull forward you had in the first quarter as importers were front-running all of the tariffs.

At this point, looking forward, the Atlanta Fed GDPNow figure-- which is a measure which is really a run rate based on economic metrics as they come out-- that's pointing to a 3% growth rate here in the third quarter. Now, our economic team thinks that's too high. They're looking for, actually, a sequential slowdown over the next couple of quarters.

So at this point for investors, I still think you should probably remain overweight small-caps overall. They trade at a 15% discount to a composite of our fair values. But I think you're going to need to have a strong hand. And if we do see some near-term downward volatility, I think you need to be able to hold those through that volatility until we really get all three of those conditions lining up-- which, if it's not now, we think probably is going to be by early to mid next year.

Greg Bonnell: That's interesting. So take a look at the small-caps area, do some more research there.

What about sectors? You mentioned the fact that the tech trade may look like it's getting played out. What specific sectors are you looking at?

David Sekera: Well, first of all is communications. That's a sector I think you and I have talked about for well over a year. It's been one of the most, if not the most, undervalued sector over the past year. In fact, I believe that was as low as a 32% undervalued level last April. And part of the reason had been because Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) and Meta (META) are well over 50% of the composite of that index by market cap. Two stocks that we had long thought had been undervalued. Meta moved up earlier this year. And now, we're seeing Alphabet up. I think Alphabet was up 9% yesterday after the court ruling.

So communications overall, probably now pretty close to fairly valued from a sector perspective, but we still see a lot of value in a lot of their traditional media and communication names. Verizon (VZ) would be one that I would highlight for investors to take a look at.

Now, thinking forward, with the Fed poised to start cutting interest rates, I would highlight real estate. That's a 7% undervalued sector. Personally, I'd still steer clear of urban office space. I don't necessarily like the risk-reward dynamics there. But for tenants that have more defensive characteristics, I think that's a good play for the next couple of months.

Healthcare, also at a 6% discount. That sector's been under a lot of pressure, may even actually be the worst-performing sector year to date. Now, within that sector, I prefer medical devices and medtech over the pharma names. But if you're going to be involved in that, I would look towards names like Bristol-Myers (BMY), which in our view, are deep value names.

And then lastly, energy. Energy trades at a 4% discount. I think it just provides a good natural hedge in your portfolio for any additional geopolitical risk or if inflation were to make a meaningful comeback. And with the Fed cutting rates over time, that should increase the demand for energy as the economy starts ramping back up. So, we see a lot of value in that sector.

Then some of the sub-sectors I'll just end with are going to be the food companies. We see a lot of consumer packaged group companies very undervalued. And then home builders, building products should also do well with the Fed cutting rates. And unfortunately, the defense industry is still a long tailwind behind that one. See a lot of value there.

Greg Bonnell: What is the greatest risk to your thesis, then? Is it the Fed is simply not playing along with the market expectations?

David Sekera: Well, that would certainly be a big risk to the market here in the short term. The market is pricing in not only the one rate cut in September, but two, and now it looks like even up to three rate cuts this year. So if they were not to cut rates, that would be a big surprise to the market, and I think that would end up hurting a lot of these sectors that we think are undervalued today because we are looking for those rate cuts.

Original Post

data.title: Moody's: The Perpetual Cash Flow Machine
data.description:
The perpetual cash flow box

Let’s say you are casually hiking in the Nevada dessert one day, near Area 51, and you come by a strange looking box. It’s a large box, but it fits in the back of the truck and definitely looks "out of this world". You carefully take it home and ask your scientist friend to look it over.

After a few days he returns it and tells you the box is something of a "small hadron collider", powered by an even smaller fusion core and is likely used to transmute elements. He tested it and he was able to turn a few ounces of lead into gold!

Why did he return it? Because he is your friend and quite an honest guy.

If you don’t believe this kind of alchemy is possible, see here. Given enough resources (like an unlimited power source modelled from the stars), everything is possible.

Naturally you both focus on the new “business venture”. You spend $2,000 to buy a Ton of lead and store it in your garage (the F-150 goes on the street and the minivan stays put – you’ve learned things).

Because you both are “blind” to the inner workings of the box, it is a trial-and-error process. For all your efforts you can only transmute 10 ounces per month. That seems to be the design capacity of the machine.

Somewhat disappointed, you cancel the order for the second delivery of lead. The 32,000 ounces you already have will last you for 3,200 months (over 250 years).

Still, 120 ounces each year at $3,000/oz is $360,000 – you can both quit your day jobs. However, after a while, you realize the box is too valuable and may get in the way of your friendship. You offer to build a shed out back to secure it, and to buy out your friend's half share at a fair price.

So the next problem is how to value this alien gift. You dust off the finance 101 textbook and find a formula for a “perpetuity”, which looks applicable to value "forever cash flows" and is simple enough that even non-scientists can grasp it:

  • Perpetuity: Free cash flow (or dividend) / Discount Rate

Since there are no operating expenses or cost of goods sold (the cost of lead, at $0.06 per ounce is irrelevant), and no salesforce needed; selling gold is the easiest thing! The $360,000 revenue is all profit. We'll be ignoring taxes for simplicity - you promise yourself you’ll pay the tax once you figure out how to tell the government how you found this thing.

Although the machine can run forever, there are still risks (that the original owner shows up, for instance or - less frightening- that the price of gold drops).

Since the alternative to investing in this venture is to invest in “the equity of other ventures” you decide to use the average total return of the S&P 500 as a proxy for the opportunity cost of capital – which is close to 10%.

Thus, the little box is worth $360k/10% or $3.6 million. Don’t worry if you can’t come up with $1.8 million to pay your scientist buddy, the purchase may be funded with revenue from gold sales, it will take you some 5 years to pay him, then the whole thing is all yours. You are going back to work (for now)!

A perpetuity with growth

The next day, you find a previously unseen button and press it. The result is some kind of optimization in its software. Every time the machine is used, something (perhaps alien AI) improves the throughput, and you get a few fractions of an ounce more every month. Let’s say you calculate the growth to be 5% per year.

Luckily, there is a “perpetuity with growth” in the next page of the textbook. The formula is essentially the same, but with a slight variation:

Growth Perpetuity:

  • Free Cash flow x (1+g) / (Discount rate – g), where "g" is the growth rate.

Plugging in the numbers: $360,000 x 1.05 / (10% - 5%) = $378,000 / 5% or $7.6 million.

Because you too are honest, you tell your friend you will pay twice as much as a result of the discovery of the growth button, and you shake on the new price.

Back to Earth

Perpetuities are useful valuation tools but need to be used carefully. Very few companies offer such long cash flow visibility. The most recent example is the “AI capex race” unfolding at top tech companies – who could have seen that coming? Or if they say they saw it coming... gotten the sheer size and timing correct?

More typically, competition does away with perpetual cash flows, or some regulatory or technological development.

Moody’s (NYSE:MCO) is one potential exception. In some respects it has about as much visibility for cash flow as our alien tiny hadron collider. No matter what gets thrown at them, including a "Great Financial Crisis" or GFC, growth has always been strong in rolling 5-year periods (more on that to follow).

Management explains their growth algorithm the following way, as it applies to their ratings division:

The growth algorithm

As implied by the chart above, the company has many tailwinds that are likely to persist in the foreseeable future. Fixed income markets, whether private or public, will grow together with economies and their companies. Disintermediation of lending, M&A, IPO’s and developing capital markets abroad are all trends that favor rating agencies.

It is not a stretch to think of them like tollbooth operators for access to the capital markets. Rated issuers enjoy lower rates on their bonds but also indirect benefits from the implied "halo of approval". An investment-grade company, for example, will see their shares trade higher, due to their improved market access and reduced liquidity risks. Suppliers may relax credit terms as well. If the issuer is a regulated company, like a utility or insurer, the regulator will sleep better at night.

Moody’s captures only a few basis points of issuance costs, an amount so small that most issuers elect to have more than one rating (from competitors S&P Global or Fitch), and many go for all three.

This highlights the competitive dynamics at play here. They technically operate in an oligopoly; however, since most “buyers” pick two out of three services, the pricing behavior is more like yearly price adjustments that at least match but often exceed CPI.

Add to those price increases an ever growing “pie” as economies expand and take debt markets along with them – we are talking nominal growth here - and you can see how there is essentially no limit to growth. Another factor is the “deepening” of capital markets in emerging jurisdictions… which is another smaller but enduring tailwind at play.

The moat is partly regulatory; Moody's is one of the main Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organizations (NRSRO). But it is more than that. It takes time to build a track record on rating 30-year bonds, for example. So Moody's +100 years in business is not easy to replicate fast. More fundamentally, though, it is all about reputation, and they enjoy top marks in that regard, not only in the US, but internationally as well.

All very well, but in practice, does the algorithm really work?

“Back testing” the algorithm:

Following is the (roughly 25-year) track record since Moody’s was spun out of Dunn & Bradstreet in the late 1990’s.

Revenue growth has been impressive, to say the least. Despite some yearly drops coinciding with recessions, when looking at rolling 5-year periods, revenue has always grown.

More importantly, let's see how close historical growth tracked the 6%-9% range (highlighted in yellow below) taken from management's growth algorithm:

If you plan to hold the company for more than five years (and especially if you plan for longer), the growth target is very likely to be "hit" - despite short term cyclicality. Going by the ten-year rolling window, it has never gone below the 6% floor, even during the GFC period.

Multiples are high, but are they still reasonable?

There is no certainty in this “game,” only degrees of confidence. Let’s imagine there are only two realms, the reasonable price area and the unreasonable. Where does the stock price (of about $480 currently) belong?

One way to approach the question is to apply the perpetuity formula to our current earnings and use different growth and discount rate assumptions:

As highlighted by the boxed numbers, the current price seems to imply growth rates of 7% to 9% and discount rates of 10% to 12%. The discount rates exceed historic market returns (not to mention expected ones) and MCO is a higher than average quality business, so there is some cushion there.

As for growth, remember we are discounting growing earnings, not revenue. Earnings growth will be helped along by things like operating leverage and stock buybacks, which over time are meaningful levers.

For example:

Using actual EPS growth in the perpetuity formula breaks it down. Valuation results tend toward infinity as growth rates approach discount rates and then turn negative, which renders the formula useless for such cases.

All this is to say that there is some “margin of safety” in using revenue growth instead of EPS growth in our use of the perpetuity formula. In short, the results of the simple valuation formula point to the price (near all time highs) still being very much reasonable.

The other test we can run to answer the question is to pick an “equally expensive” point back in time and see how the poor fellow that bought the stock that day did subsequently.

As you can see, the prior peak valuation was in 2007, at over 12x revenues and very close to where the multiple sits today. Having bought at that then-all-time-high price of about $70 still resulted in a 7X stock return over the following 18 years (or about 13% CAGR including dividends).

We know (but our not-so-poor-now fellow didn't) that back in mid-2007 the very severe GFC was just around the corner. The GFC was very painful to rating agencies in particular – it would have been hard to hold on. And yet, for those who did (Buffett included), the payoff was above-market returns, even from that worst possible high-looking price.

The foregoing seems to imply that the current price could be considered reasonable even if we have another GFC right in front of us! Hopefully we don't; I don’t think we do, but I also did not know back in 2007 how bad things would get.

Both approaches seem to point to the current stock price falling into the reasonable realm. I could not argue convincingly that it fits in the other. To do that, we would have to assume that growth will slow down permanently… but how?

Risks

GFC 2.0, dismissed above as near, is always a non-zero probability, which increases with time. Financially-driven crises are particularly acute. Both S&P Global (SPGI) and Moody's have diversified their businesses somewhat to withstand another bad one (both by seeking to secure recurring revenues). MCO has done better with Moody's Analytics, a segment that matches 9% growth from ratings and is 95% recurring (although MA will never reach the same 60% margins). Still, as the years following 2008 proved, it is very hard to grow when capital markets are digesting past excesses through deleveraging.

Although it is hard to see them repeat past mistakes, reputation damage is still the main risk. Lessons were learned after the subprime ratings mishaps, and regulation was tweaked, but the companies retained their moats and have proceeded to diversify their businesses. If they survived the likely worst-case scenario back then, they are on stronger footing today.

Technology has been more of a friend than a foe for rating agencies. I believe this will continue to be the case, as their large and proprietary databases only become more valuable through new perspectives enabled by AI, machine learning, etc.

Finally, although the high-looking current price is likely to yield market-beating returns, in the near term, some stagnation or drop is possible. This is more likely if we see risk premiums rise and debt issuance slow down - the stock will fall even if we don't enter any recession (which is just what happened early in the second quarter).

Takeaway

The rating agency moat is so good I own both SPGI and MCO. Moody's trades at a slight premium to SPGI which I feel is warranted. S&P's MI segment is growing at only 5% or so, and has become a revenue source equal to their ratings segment, but with lower margins.

MCO has a ratings franchise just as large (see here for a comparison of both) and overall better margins as their diversifying efforts have higher growth and smaller share of revenues.

Although Moody's looks expensive at near all-time highs, the basic observation here is that the market systematically undervalues high-growth businesses that also possess unassailable moats. In other words, these few companies tend to yield market beating results from almost any price. Therefore, until we break out substantially into unknown valuation territory (say 15x revenues) or something comes along that points to a weakening of the moat... MCO continues to look like a strong buy.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Dingdong: Consider Both External And Internal Tailwinds (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I've turned "Neutral" on Dingdong (Cayman) Limited (NYSE:DDL). The increasingly intensive rivalry in China's online commerce sector will be a drag on DDL's near-term revenues. But it's trying to enhance margins over a longer time horizon with business restructuring efforts.

DDL's above-consensus 3Q2024 bottom line and management's optimistic expectations of 4Q24 results were detailed in the earlier Nov. 12, 2024 update.

Competitive Pressures Are Reflected In The Financials

In its FY24 20-F filing, DDL cautioned that growing threats from "other fresh grocery e-commerce players" and "other Internet companies in mainland China" could possibly "reduce our pricing power and market share." This is a risk that has unfortunately materialized.

SA News reported in June 2025 that JD.com, Inc. (JD) has begun to "compete with Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF) (OTCPK:MPNGY) in (new) areas including food delivery" by "offering significant user subsidies." Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) also indicated at its late-August analyst briefing that it recently "made an additional large investment to address the severe short-term lack of (food) delivery capacity."

Imagine you are a Chinese consumer who has to decide between cooking or ordering food online. An increase in delivery platforms' incentives means it becomes cheaper to have meals at your doorstep. When fixing dinner by yourself turns into a less attractive option, this will likely translate into a lower frequency of grocery purchases to DDL's detriment.

Dingdong should also find it difficult to keep pace with its rivals, considering the differences in scale. The firm's trailing 12-month topline is less than 3% of the same for MPNGF, which is the smallest of the three competitors highlighted earlier. DDL doesn't have a sufficiently wide revenue base to absorb additional expenses linked to promotions and discounting.

It isn't just about food delivery being a substitute for home-cooked meals; there's direct competition as well. Meituan's Aug. 27 results call revealed that it is "accelerating the expansion" of its "self-operated on-demand grocery business" with the intention to be present in all "first- and second-tier (Chinese) cities" over time.

DDL's year-on-year sales growth moderated from +16% in 2Q24 and +9% in 1Q25 to +7% for the most recent three-month period.

It's fair to think that Dingdong is ceding ground to the big boys due to the above-mentioned challenges. I forecast that its topline growth will halve from 15.5% last year to below 8% in FY25.

But Key Operating Ratios Suggest That Self-Help Measures Are Working

External events are not within Dingdong's control. The PRC's technology giants have shown the desire to venture into new business areas, and this has hurt niche players like DDL. But the enterprise is helping its own cause with fresh internal initiatives.

DDL disclosed in its Aug. 21 media release that the firm's "4G strategy centered on good users, good products, good service, and good mindshare had been in place for six months."

There will certainly be people in China who are less cost-conscious and place a greater emphasis on non-pricing factors. It makes sense for Dingdong to target this particular segment more aggressively with moves such as the "4G" approach.

"Good users" are defined as those "who value product quality" and are "willing to pay for it," according to DDL's 2Q25 analyst meeting disclosures. As per its results slides, this group of clients increased 19% sequentially in the latest quarter, representing 28% of platform-wide users.

Dingdong is also tweaking its assortment. Its total "Gross Merchandise Value" or "GMV" contributed by "good products" (as per the company's categorization) went up 21 ppts (percentage points) quarter-on-quarter to 43% in the second quarter this year.

Shortening the time needed for goods to reach customers is an important component of "good service." The Apr-Jun '25 period saw DDL record its lowest proportion of orders that were sent late in history at 2.7%.

On the other hand, "good mindshare" is all about making the act of shopping at Dingdong's platform a habit. The average user made at least 4 purchases every 30 days; "good users" ordered on the company's website almost twice as often in a month's time.

In my opinion, it will be unwise for DDL to be market share- or volume-focused and compete with JD, MPNGF, and BABA on price.

It's far more logical to enhance profitability at the expense of revenue shrinkage. My estimates point to its operating margin widening by +50 bps in the next three years to 2.0% with the adoption of the "4G strategy."

My Conclusion

I'm downgrading Dingdong's rating from a "Buy" previously to a "Hold" now.

The competitive landscape in China's food market has changed substantially, and sub-scale operators are at a huge disadvantage now. I can't convince myself to be bullish on DDL anymore.

But the stock isn't deserving of a "Sell." Dingdong is reorganizing its internal operations to meet the needs of a less price-sensitive client segment.

Also, I see DDL's shares as fairly valued. Its consensus "price-to-free cash flow" or "P/FCF" multiple for the next 12 months is 7.8 times, or 18% below JD's 9.5x. A high-teens percentage discount for the disparity in size is reasonable.

The other two are not considered appropriate peers for the purpose of valuation. MPNGF is loss-making, while BABA has non-ecommerce divisions like cloud and fintech services.

data.title: 5 Clear Signs Of A Market Bubble
data.description:
The market continues to trade near all-time highs across all the major indexes. Equities continue to climb despite a clearly slowing jobs market. Job openings in this week's JOLTs report were lower than the number of unemployed individuals in the U.S. This is the first time this has occurred since April of 2021, over four years ago. Before the bell on Friday, the August BLS report showed only 22,000 positions created last month. Less than one third the consensus estimate of 75,000. This followed a disappointing July BLS reading as well as significant downward revisions to May and June jobs numbers.

Yet, the market continues remain near record levels. The rally appears to be primarily driven by the huge investments tech titans like Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN) and Meta Platforms (META) are making to build massive data centers and other AI related infrastructure and capabilities as well on increasing hopes that the Federal Reserve will finally start to reduce interest rates starting with the upcoming FOMC in mid-September.

So, how overvalued are U.S. equities? Significantly, in my opinion. In today's column, I look at five charts/metrics that should give any prudent person significant pause.

The U.S. makes up approximately 4.2% of the global population yet U.S. equities make up nearly half of all global stock market capitalization. Yes, New York City is the global financial capital and Silicon Valley is the epicenter of technology including the AI revolution. The greenback is the global reserve currency, at least for the moment. And the U.S. military is considered the preeminent military force on the planet.

However, do U.S. equities deserve a market capitalization over 5.5 times that of the European Union, which has over 100 million more citizens? Does it merit over five times the market capitalization of China with some 1.4 billion people?

I often point out the S&P 500 (SP500) trades at approximately 3.2 times revenues, which has only been exceeded briefly at the end of the Internet Boom.

However, this valuation looks downright attractive compared to the price to sales ratio of the NASDAQ (COMP:IND), which is now north 6.7 times.

Then we have the market cap to GDP ratio, also known as the "Buffett Indicator," given the weight the Oracle of Omaha gave to it during it his long, illustrious investing career. Is it any wonder why Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) has built up record cash holdings in recent years before Mr. Buffett's retirement this year?

Let's move over to S&P 500 earnings yield (trailing 12-month earnings/S&P Index) which is just over 3.3%. The yield on the "risk free" 10-Year Treasury (US10Y) is currently 4.08%. This means, not only is there no "risk premium" to holding equities, it is actually negative.

Finally, we can look at the overall market via several arithmetic means. The chart above was calculated at the end of August. It consists of:

  • The Crestmont Research P/E ratio.
  • The cyclical P/E ratio using the trailing 10-year earnings as the divisor.
  • The Q ratio, which is the total price of the market divided by its replacement cost.
  • The relationship of the S&P composite price to a regression trendline.

These calculations show the market has never been valued higher.

An investor can believe one of two things with the markets trading at these levels. They can put their faith in the five of the most dangerous words in the English language "It is different this time," or acknowledge that the market is trading in extremely overbought territory.

I lean clearly to the latter conclusion. That leaves my portfolio roughly one quarter allocated in short term treasuries yielding four percent. And the rest is within covered call holdings around the few stocks in this market sporting reasonable valuations. That provides some downside risk mitigation. It also allows me to continue to participate in the rally should it continue before reality eventually arrives.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Vs Eli Lilly: Novo A Clear Winner
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Without a doubt, there are some great opportunities in the healthcare market right now.

But not all stocks are created equal, and in my opinion, Novo Nordisk (NVO) is arguably the best pharma stock you can own right now.

Many investors favour Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), but I will argue in this report that Novo outperforms Lilly in almost every aspect, while trading at a significantly lower price.

Let’s jump right in.

Product And Pipeline

Novo and Lilly are in competition in many different areas of the market, but the focus in recent years has been on weight loss drugs. Novo Nordisk has Ozempic and Wegovy, while Lilly has Mounjaro and Zepbound.

We can't deny that Eli Lilly showed some early superiority in this market, but all the recent data seems to suggest Novo may have the superior product now.

Wegovy has just been proven to be superior to Zepbound at reducing heart disease. Wegovy is also the first weight-loss medicine with an FDA label to reduce major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE).

On top of that, we know Novo also has approval to launch its own oral form of Wegovy.

In terms of future prospects, I won’t deny that Eli Lilly looks like it has a better pipeline.

Eli Lilly’s pipeline combines metabolic innovation with broad diversification. The key asset is orforglipron, an oral GLP-1 showing strong Phase 3 weight-loss data, with approvals expected from 2025 onward.

Lilly is also advancing donanemab for Alzheimer's, mirikizumab for IBD, pirtobrutinib in blood cancers, and lepodisiran, which dramatically lowers lipoprotein(a).

The company also has strategic deals like the Superluminal Medicines partnership and Verve’s CRISPR-based PCSK9 therapy to further expand its obesity and cardiovascular reach.

Novo Nordisk’s pipeline is more focused, centred on obesity and diabetes.

Flagship programs include CagriSema, showing ~23% weight loss in Phase 3, and amycretin, a next-gen GLP-1/amylin agonist with early strong results.

A high-dose oral Wegovy could become the first oral GLP-1 approved for obesity and CV risk reduction.

Novo also licensed a triple-agonist and struck a $2.2B deal with Septerna for GPCR-based therapies. Beyond GLP-1s, it’s adding insulin icodec, a once-weekly basal insulin, and concizumab, a haemophilia treatment—underscoring its strategy of deepening its metabolic moat while expanding selectively into adjacent areas.

All in all, Lilly is more diversified, but I don't think this is enough to justify the difference in valuation, especially when we look at growth and profitability metrics.

Growth And Profitability

Let’s start by looking at growth.

Yes, it is true that Eli Lilly has been growing more in the last year, with a whopping 36% revenue growth. However, if we expand into the last 3 and 5 years, it seems like growth has been quite similar.

The question here is whether Lilly can maintain this lead. The market seems to be betting hard that it will, but that is a risky bet in my opinion.

The downside here is that LLY is being more aggressive with its spending, and this is taking a toll on profitability.

NVO has a higher net income margin and generates almost $9 billion more in cash from operations.

This is perhaps one of the most compelling reasons to own Novo over Lilly.

Valuation

This then brings us to valuation, the other compelling reason to own Novo.

Novo trades at under half the FY1 P/E of LLY. EV/EBITDA is also much more attractive, and most of all, there’s a wild difference between the Price/Cash Flow.

Novo actually pays a healthy dividend, giving investors a nice 3% yield.

At these prices, the stock is undervalued, not just compared to Eli Lilly, but also when compared to the broader market.

I would attribute two main reasons to the discount versus Lilly.

  • Lilly is more aggressively pursuing growth

  • Novo is not an American company

But what investors see now as a drawback, they may soon begin to see as an advantage.

Novo’s Discount Could Become A Premium

The way I see it, Novo is the better buy, not just because of the discount, but indeed because of the reasons that it is discounted.

The fact that Novo is being more conservative with its spending and balance sheet will become a strength if we see a recession or market correction. Investors are pursuing growth aggressively now, but when the tides turn, the higher-flying names like Lilly will come down harder, while Novo will provide more stability.

On the other hand, investors have always placed a premium on American companies, but this is slowly changing. European stocks have seen strong gains and inflows in the last 12 months.

Global investors are seeking diversification from US assets and the US dollar, and with yields finally rising in Europe, capital is starting to flow its way.

This is a trend that may take years to develop, but ultimately, I think global portfolios are underweight European equities, and this is slowly changing.

Novo could become a big beneficiary, and in my opinion, this could naturally raise its valuation.

Risks

There are still risks to consider. Novo is going through an important transition, with a new CEO taking charge.

There’s also the issue of tariffs, which could definitely affect Novo. Even though Novo is not a US company, over half of its revenues come from the US. The current administration could certainly get in the way of Novo’s growth here.

Final Thoughts

In conclusion, I still think Novo is the clear winner between the two stocks and an overall great buy.

In the long run, this stock will recover and do very well, in my opinion.

data.title: The Fed's Beige Book Says It's Time To Invest For Stagflation
data.description:
There's been an increasing number of signs that suggest we are entering a period of stagflation. This is a very challenging dynamic for the Federal Reserve to grapple with, and the last time we had a serious stagflation issue was back in the 1970s. The stagflation in the 1970s was marked with challenging economic problems, including the 2 main pillars of stagflation, which are stagnant economic growth combined with persistently high inflation. There can also be other economic challenges during periods of stagflation, and this includes rising and/or high unemployment. The Federal Reserve's August Beige Book appeared to confirm stagflationary conditions. Let's take a closer look below at stagflation and review what assets do well in this environment and which ones typically do poorly.

What Drove Stagflation In The 1970s?

One big issue driving stagflation was the 1973 OPEC oil embargo, which caused a surge in oil prices, which led to higher production and transportation costs in the U.S.A. This surge in oil prices caused many companies to raise prices, which created inflation, and at the same time, many companies cut jobs, which led to rising unemployment. Many economists believe that the period of stagflation in the 1970s was also caused by expansionist monetary and fiscal policies that occurred in the late 1960s and early 1970s since the U.S. Government was trying to stimulate the economy and keep unemployment low. The problem is that this led to an excessive increase in the money supply, which fueled inflation. Paul Volcker was the Fed Chair at that time, and he raised the Federal Funds Rate to 20%, which resulted in a severe recession. However, the recession reset was just what was needed in order to take inflation down and bring the labor market back to a point where it could grow again.

What Is Driving Stagflation Today?

It seems that expansionist monetary and fiscal policies that occurred just before the stagflationary period in the 1970s were repeated with all the government stimulus that took place in response to the Covid pandemic. That seems to have contributed significantly to the rise in inflation, which was once labeled as being transitory. In response to this surge in inflation, the Federal Reserve raised interest rates, which finally seems to have reduced inflationary pressures. However, inflation is still near 3%, which is well above the Fed's target of 2%. In addition, there is concern that inflation could start to rise again due to the tariffs implemented by the Trump Administration. Another concern is that if the Fed goes forward with a rate cut in September, this could result in more inflation in the coming months. This could especially be an issue if the Fed cuts rates at a time when many asset prices are at or near record highs, which includes home prices and the stock market.

The Challenges For The Federal Reserve

Inflation has improved, but it could be reignited by rate cuts as well as tariffs. This desire to reduce inflation has to be balanced with another Fed mandate, which is to keep unemployment low. The current Consumer Price Index, or "CPI," is 2.7%, which is well above the Fed's target, and another CPI report will be released on September 11, 2025. With these inflation readings still being higher than the Fed's target level, it will be challenging to make any additional progress on inflation if the Fed decides to lower rates in September. It will also be challenging to make any further progress on inflation since many companies are starting to raise prices due to the cost increases that are being fueled by tariffs. Walmart (WMT) and other major retailers have said tariff costs are rising each week, and it plans to raise prices in response. I believe the impact of tariffs is just starting to trickle through the economy, so we have not yet felt the full impact, and this could reignite inflation in the coming months. Some people suggest that tariffs are "just" a one-time cost increase, but even if that is true, any cost increase still results in higher prices, and in addition to that, higher prices typically result in reduced demand, and that could lead to a stagnating economy.

The economy is slowing, and this can be seen by looking at the jobs market, manufacturing data, and more. This suggests we are potentially entering a period of stagnation, which, combined with persistent or rising inflation, is leading us to stagflation. The U.S. manufacturing sector has been contracting, and the ISM Manufacturing Index hit 48 in July, which represented the 5th consecutive monthly contraction and the worst decline since October 2024.

On August 1, the Bureau of Labor Statistics released the latest jobs report, which showed that July non-farm payroll growth was just 73,000. This was significantly below the expectations for 110,000. There was even worse news because the payrolls for May and June were revised down by a total of 258,000. The combination of this economic weakness along with persistent inflation that could rise as tariff expenses kick in points towards a period of stagflation.

The September 5th jobs report only confirms the recent shift in the labor market and seems to confirm a weakening trend. Expectations were at 75,000, but U.S. nonfarm payrolls rose by only 22,000 in August. Furthermore, unemployment rose to 4.3% from 4.2%. I believe this extremely weak jobs report opens the door to a negative payrolls number in the coming months, and this could spark a major growth scare for stock market investors and possibly lead to a recession.

These Assets Typically Outperform During Periods Of Stagflation

Hard assets and other raw materials such as gold, commodities, copper, and oil can act as a hedge against inflation and stagflation. Agricultural products also typically outperform in periods of stagflation. Defensive stocks such as consumer staples, utilities, healthcare, and value stocks tend to outperform as well. Investors tend to flock towards value stocks and companies that have pricing power and ones that provide products and services that are basic needs. By contrast, high-growth stocks and long-term bonds tend to underperform.

Gold has been surging, so I feel like it is a little late to buy now. However, platinum still appears undervalued, and I would buy this and other precious metals on pullbacks. I am finding buying opportunities in beaten-down value and healthcare stocks. This includes Centene (CNC), recently written about in this article, as well as UnitedHealth Group (UNH), in a separate article.

In Summary

I don't like the stock market in general now due to high valuations, so I would be a seller overall. However, I do think there are pockets of buying opportunities that continue to develop, and I am adding exposure to assets that can perform well as stagflationary pressures potentially increase. If stagflationary pressures continue, the Fed might have its hands tied, and ultimately, an economic reset (as was the case during the stagflation that started in the 1970s) that a recession can bring might be the only real way to clear the decks.
